transcript: Ultimate Beastmaster is an athletic competition show similar to American Ninja Warrior where athletes have to get from one end of an obstacle course to the other end without falling in the disgusting water below. And the thing that makes season two really stand out from season one is that there's about 20 times the amount of rock climbers. But it's not all rock climbers because a secret challenger did show up for season 2 that we didn't really see in Season 1. Parkour athletes. Parkour! What was supposed to be Season 2 of Ultimate Beastmaster really turned into rock climbing versus parkour, which, in today's day and age, is basically the same thing. Some of the really high-end and renowned parkour athletes showed up for Season 2, including the parkour world champion. But the rock climbers really brought their A-game as well,, because for the competitors in season two are IFSC rock climbers. We had Mikael and Basu Malam, Haibin Chu, and Manuel Cornu. But we also had some wacky characters show up in season two. Like there's one dude who trains with like an oxygen restricting mask on, and he swears that if anyone else wore it, they would die. So I guess he thinks he's like Bane or something. And there's also a cheerleading coach who's also a physics teacher, who I did not expect to get far at all. So starting off with level one, the first obstacle is the grinders, which are a bunch of cogs that the competitors have to jump between. And for some reason, the editors really want you to think that these things are made out of like solid steel, even though they're clearly foam, because they put this super fake metal sound effect in whenever one of the competitors lands on one. Like you can dial it back a little bit. It doesn't have to sound like two cranes slamming into each other, but nobody really falls on the grinders for the most part. All the climbers and the parkour athletes make it through easily and we move on to face plant now in season one hangman was just pretty chill it's like this platform that leans forward and then you got to jump off it but i guess the producers thought it was too easy so they just made it go down like significantly further like whenever you watch a competitor get on it you're just waiting for it to stop but it just just keeps going. But the next obstacle is the energy coils, and this is where things get really interesting. Now, they do use the same metal sound effect for the energy coils, but it's a little bit more believable this time because the falls on this are intense. Now, besides the fact that this obstacle has some really funny falls on it, it's also just a really difficult obstacle. It's probably one of the hardest obstacles in Ultimate Beastmaster. And you can definitely tell that some of the competitors watched season one because this one dude spends like two minutes debating if he wants to jump or not because he gets so scared just jump what! What are you doing? What are you doing? You're making it worse on your mind. You're making it worse, just jump. So not surprisingly, the parkour athletes like destroy the energy coils. They are without a doubt the best ones on this. They cruise through energy coils, but to my surprise, the rock climbers actually did pretty well in energy coils as well. You know, rock climbers are known for having these little spindly weak legs like they can't jump. They can't use their legs for anything. A lot of rock climbers can't even deadlift a 45 pound bar. But I guess Manuel Cornu's got hops because he flies through these coils. And you know who else easily makes it through the coils? The cheer coach. Now, I didn't go into this thinking that the cheer coach was going to be like terrible or anything. I just didn't expect him to be one of the better athletes there. Like he was one of the only athletes to get the extra point thruster on the energy coils and make it all the way through the energy coils after that. But the final obstacle in level one was the mag wall, which is just a rock climbing wall where the holds fall off intermittently behind you. Now, in season one, the mag wall is a little bit more difficult. It had a pretty long and gruesome overhang section. So in season two, they got rid of that and made it a little bit easier. So what they decided to do to balance it out was just throw this huge dino into the final platform that like nobody could do. Even Manuel Cornu, who's probably the most accomplished boulder that showed up for season two, couldn't make the jump. So all four of our pro climbers make it to level two and a huge handful of parkour athletes also make it there and this leads into what is without a doubt my favorite obstacle in ultimate beastmaster stomach churn stomach churn is basically just a bunch of spinning platforms that you have to jump between and they really dial those things up to 10. Like those things are spinning fast. They're spinning so fast that some of the competitors don't even get to jump. They just get launched off and the ones that don't get launched off and stay on it for too long get so dizzy that I'm pretty sure their eyes stop working. But this is definitely an obstacle where the parkour athletes excelled some of these athletes would absolutely destroy stomach churn and i don't think i saw anyone do it as well in all of season one but shockingly they actually weren't the best at this obstacle because somehow the cheerleading coach was one of the only two athletes that was able to get the point thruster on this and just like easily stand and jump between these i honestly didn't even think he'd make it to level two and he destroyed stomach churn but all four of our pro climbers end up also making it through We'll be right back. this and just like easily stand and jump between these. I honestly didn't even think he'd make it to level two and he destroyed stomach churn. But all four of our pro climbers end up also making it through stomach churn, although not as gracefully as a lot of the parkour athletes did, which leads us into digestive track, which is basically just a big tube you have to climb up and just pray you don't slip and drown. But it's actually at the top of digestive track where things get interesting because as the tube is still lowering, you have to jump onto a moving treadmill. I don't know why, I just find this so conceptually funny. It just sounds like something you'd hear in Jackass, like jumping onto a moving treadmill. But surprisingly, we don't have that many crazy falls on this obstacle like we did in season one. I think a lot of the competitors watched season one and saw some of the nastier falls on this. so a lot of the times they'd get up there and realize it was too low for them to jump and they would just kind of bail out instead of attempting it so it turned out that the only people that really fell on the dreadmills were the people that were jumping off it at the end and this is another obstacle where Manuel Cornu really surprised me because he was actually pretty low on his platform by the time he jumped and uh he had a pretty epic leap onto the dreadmills. And this leads into the last two obstacles of level 2, which are chain reaction and vertebrates. But chain reactions were everybody really struggled. We had very few athletes finish level 2 just because of chain reaction. Chain reaction was so difficult that even Basim Awam wasn't able to complete it, but thankfully the other three pro climbers all managed to make it all the way through to the end of level two So now we move on to level three which starts off with the classic obstacle the ejector This is another obstacle with some really funny falls because people just can't seem to time the jumps Right and a lot of times you can't really tell who's going to do well and who's not some of these really good parkour athletes Will hop on it and they'll just fall right off the end But there are occasions where you can absolutely tell when somebody stands no chance on ejector There's also this one poor dude who they just really build up before ejector We'll be right back. right off the end. But there are occasions where you can absolutely tell when somebody stands no chance on ejector. There's also this one poor dude who they just really build up before ejector. They basically go through this whole list of how far he has to get on this obstacle and all the things he has to accomplish and just how much he has ahead of him if he wants to make it to the final round. And then this happens. But just like in the previous rounds, none of our pro climbers or our high-end parkour athletes really struggle on the ejector so the final obstacle of round three is where we really start to see a lot of separation hangman requires the contestants to basically campus up like 10 or 15 rungs to get to the top and finish round three and this is where you can really tell the difference between the parkour athletes and the rock climbers because three of the four rock climbers actually managed to finish hangman and none of the parkour athletes even get close. But the thing that I found really interesting about hangman was that all three climbers who managed to send it used a completely different technique. Basim Awam was the first athlete to make it to the end of hangman and he used what's probably the most sensible technique which was figure fours. He basically figure foured every single hold so that he could statically reach to the next one and even though this took a lot longer than the ways the other athletes chose to do it it just seemed like the most consistent and safe way to get to the end. Haibin Chu was the second climber to send Hangman and he just chose to campus his way across the whole thing which does make sense because he's primarily a lead climber so he probably has a good amount more endurance than the rest of the pro climbers here. But the most interesting technique to me was Manuel Cornu's technique, because he chose to one-arm lock-off on each one so that he could statically reach to the next hold. This seemed like the most inefficient way to do it, but he made it look so easy, so I guess he's just really good at one-arm lock-offs because he did this for every single one and made it all the way to the end of Hangman. So at the end of all these obstacles, the top two athletes are chosen to compete in level four for the title of ultimate beastmaster. And in almost every single episode, the final two athletes are a parkour athlete and a rock climber. I don't know what the odds are of this happening, but in literally seven out of the nine episodes, this is the final matchup. And just like in season one, level four heavily favorited rock climbers. Now, even though it wasn't just a straightforward rock wall like it was in season one, it was really just a more complicated version of a rock wall that involved a lot more campusing. And for the most part, things were actually pretty neck and neck for almost every episode because the first obstacle really favored parkour athletes, but the second obstacle was really just a bunch of campusing. But it was really the third obstacle that absolutely destroyed the parkour athletes. Motherboard was an absolutely massive pegboard that the athletes had to campus their way up to the top of. Motherboard was so grueling that even the climbers really struggled on this one. And it was because of this obstacle alone that every single final ended with the rock climber winning. So in seven out of nine episodes, the final showdown is between a rock climber and a parkour athlete, and in seven out of nine episodes, the rock climber wins purely because of Motherboard. The only two exceptions to this are an obstacle course veteran who ends up making it all the way to the final obstacle of round four, and most surprising of all, the cheer coach. Not only did the cheer coach make it to level 4, but he absolutely dominated levels 1-3 and almost finished level 4. Like I know they do some wacky stuff in cheerleading, but it must be pretty intense if this guy was able to compete this well. But what I really want to focus on from level 4 is the 3 competitors who actually finished the entire course. The first one to top it was Haibin Chu, who got there in four minutes and 58 seconds. Manuel Corny was the second one to get there, and he got there one second faster in four minutes and 57 seconds. But the competitor who really dominated level four was Mikael Mouam, because he got up there so fast that they didn't even show the clock, and he had time to sit and wait for his brother to get to the top so that they could push the button together. So the finale for season two of Ultimate Beast Master has all the winners from the previous nine episodes compete in the obstacle course one more time for the title of Ultimate Beast Master. And I think the favorite going into this was definitely Mikhail Milam just because of how good his performance was and because he was able to finish level four significantly faster than the other two athletes. But then this happened. So shockingly, Mikael actually got out in the first round just because he messed up the jump on the energy coils. And you can tell he was pretty upset about this because he had easily gotten through energy coils last time. And I think he just didn't land the way he wanted to. But things played out pretty similarly to how they did in the previous episodes. And our final four that made it to round three were Haibin Chu, Manuel Cornu, the obstacle course veteran, and believe it or not, the cheer coach. I kept waiting for this guy to slip up or for it to be really obvious that it was pro athletes versus a cheer coach, but he just casually made it through the entire course every single time. It didn't even look like it was that hard for him. I don't know what this guy's doing, but he's doing something right but I think everybody kind of went into level three knowing that Haibin Chu and Manuel Cornyu were going to be the final two just because of the hangman obstacle you can even see that when Haibin Chu makes it to the start of hangman the cheer coach already like knows he's done he just like accepts defeat because he knows he's going to make it to the top so both Haibin Chu and and Manuel Cornu finish level three again and are the final two competitors for the title of Ultimate Beastmaster. Now I actually found this final to be really interesting just because Manuel Cornu is primarily a boulderer and Haibin Chu is primarily a lead climber. So it's kind of an interesting showdown of different specializations of rock climbing and it led to a pretty intense finale. Both of these competitors are climbers, so this is right in their element. But unlike the first time they ran level four, they're rushing up the motherboard. But in the end, Haibin Chu's lead climbing experience definitely came out to help him because he seemed to have a little bit more endurance, which helped him get to the top just before Manuel Cornu. So in the end, Haibin Chu was the ultimate beastmaster, and the Chinese hosts get really excited that Hai Ben-Chu won, which makes sense. But one of them is like genuinely in tears, like sobbing that he won. Like it is still just a game show. Like you can relax a little bit. Like I've never seen Gordon Ramsay break out in tears when somebody wins Hell's Kitchen. And I think my main takeaway from season two is that, uh, I really need to get into cheerleading. We'll be right back.
